docs.title: SuperCodeMode
docs.text:
SuperCodeMode helps you optimize Code Mode behavior with GEPA and run it on any backend.

Optimize Code Mode with GEPA. Run anywhere.

It helps MCP and HTTP tool users improve quality by tuning client side instruction text and routing policy without tying users to a specific runtime provider.

docs.title: What You Need to Bring (Real Usage)
docs.text:
SuperCodeMode is most useful when you already have:

  • an MCP server or Code Mode runtime (Cloudflare, local, UTCP, internal)
  • a small dataset of real tasks
  • a scoring rule (metric) for success
  • a client config where you can apply optimized prompt/Code Mode settings

Read guides/from-demo-to-real-workflow.md for the practical workflow.

docs.title: What This Project Solves
docs.text:
Many tool systems fail because the client logic is weak, even when tools are good.

Typical failures:

  • execute tool used too early
  • discovery step skipped
  • weak execution instructions
  • unstable or noisy final answers

SuperCodeMode gives you a repeatable loop to improve these behaviors.

docs.title: Who This Is For
docs.text:
• Cloudflare Code Mode MCP users
  • MCP users running search and execute style tools
  • platform engineers and evaluation teams

docs.title: What Is Included
docs.text:
• MCP stdio runner for local workflows
  • MCP streamable HTTP runner for direct Cloudflare MCP
  • HTTP runner for Cloudflare or bridge endpoints
  • local, Docker, and Monty execution backend options in demo MCP server
  • doctor checks for setup confidence
  • artifacts for run outputs and best candidates
  • observability output in JSONL and OTLP modes

docs.title: How GEPA Changes Work
docs.text:
This repo demonstrates the GEPA adapter approach where:

  • GEPA optimizes client text policy
  • runners execute tools on MCP or HTTP runtimes
  • transport can switch without rewriting adapter logic

Read guides/gepa-adapter.md for the exact behavior and commands.

docs.title: What Is Not Included By Default
docs.text:
• automatic server code mutation
  • automatic server deploy workflows

docs.title: Expected Benefits
docs.text:
• better tool routing quality
  • better final answer consistency
  • fewer unnecessary tool calls
  • easier comparison of baseline vs tuned behavior
  • easier debugging with traces and telemetry

docs.title: Left Menu Section Guide
docs.text:
Use the left menu in this order:

  1. Getting Started for install and first run
  2. Examples for runnable scenarios
  3. CLI and Config for all flags and environment settings
  4. Operations for checks, observability, and troubleshooting

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
pip install -e .
scm doctor
scm showcase --runner mcp-http
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio

Cloudflare MCP often requires auth:

scm showcase --runner mcp-http --auth-bearer "$CODEMODE_TOKEN"

docs.title: Recommended Next Commands
docs.text:
scm optimize --runner mcp-stdio --max-metric-calls 10 --save-artifact
scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl showcase --runner mcp-stdio

docs.title: Useful Pages
docs.text:
• install: getting-started/install.md
  • first run: getting-started/first-run.md
  • examples: guides/examples.md
  • from demo to real workflow: guides/from-demo-to-real-workflow.md
  • gepa adapter: guides/gepa-adapter.md
  • cloudflare http: guides/cloudflare-http.md
  • cli reference: reference/cli.md
  • observability: operations/observability.md

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This section is for first time users.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
By the end of this section you will:

  • install SuperCodeMode
  • run preflight checks
  • execute first local optimization flow
  • understand results and artifact output

docs.title: Required Background
docs.text:
• basic Python command line usage
  • basic idea of tool calling workflows

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
1. Install
  2. First Run
  3. Teen Guide

docs.title: If You Are Stuck
docs.text:
Go to:

  • Troubleshooting
  • Doctor

docs.title: First Run
docs.text:
This page shows the shortest path to a successful run.

Important:

  • these are smoke tests (they prove setup and transport work)
  • they are not your real workflow optimization yet
  • for real usage, see guides/from-demo-to-real-workflow.md

docs.title: Step 1: Run Local Showcase
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio

What you should see:

  • baseline score output
  • tuned score output
  • tool traces for each dataset item

docs.title: Step 2: Run Cloudflare MCP Showcase
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-http

This uses default endpoint https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp. Use --endpoint only if you want another MCP server. Cloudflare MCP usually requires auth, so you may need:

scm showcase --runner mcp-http --auth-bearer "$CODEMODE_TOKEN"

docs.title: Step 3: Save Artifact
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio --save-artifact

You will get artifact_path in output.

docs.title: Step 4: Run Optimization
docs.text:
scm optimize --runner mcp-stdio --max-metric-calls 10

This returns best score and best candidate text.

docs.title: What You Need for Real Workflows
docs.text:
To use SuperCodeMode in your own workflow, you will need:

  • your MCP server (Cloudflare, local, UTCP, or internal)
  • a small dataset of real tasks
  • a metric that reflects success for your use case
  • a client config where you can apply the optimized prompt/Code Mode settings

Next: guides/from-demo-to-real-workflow.md

docs.title: Step 5: Enable Observability
docs.text:
scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl showcase --runner mcp-stdio

Now you can inspect run events in artifacts/obs.jsonl.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • pip or uv
  • optional Docker Desktop for Docker backend

docs.title: Install Package (CLI)
docs.text:
With pip:

pip install supercodemode

With uv (tool install):

uv tool install supercodemode

With uv (current environment):

uv pip install supercodemode

docs.title: Optional Extras
docs.text:
Monty executor backend:

pip install "supercodemode[monty]"
uv pip install "supercodemode[monty]"

Observability integrations (Logfire, MLflow, LangSmith, Langfuse):

pip install "supercodemode[observability]"
uv pip install "supercodemode[observability]"

docs.title: Install Package (Local Development)
docs.text:
With pip:

pip install -e .

With uv:

uv pip install -e .

This installs the CLI command scm.

docs.title: Verify Install
docs.text: scm --help

docs.title: Check Environment
docs.text:
scm doctor

If you only want a quick check without Docker image run:

scm doctor --no-docker-run

docs.title: Teen Guide (Beginner Friendly)
docs.text: If you know basic Python, you can run everything here.

docs.title: Think of SuperCodeMode Like This
docs.text:
You have a robot assistant with two powers:

  • search style tool to understand what is available
  • execute style tool to run code and produce results

The robot often makes bad choices. SuperCodeMode trains the robot text instructions so it chooses better.

docs.title: Simple Mental Model
docs.text:
1. Give a question.
  2. Robot picks a tool.
  3. We score if answer is good.
  4. GEPA updates text instructions.
  5. Repeat with small budget.

docs.title: Start in 3 Commands
docs.text:
pip install -e .
scm doctor
scm showcase --runner mcp-http

This Cloudflare mode uses https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp by default.

docs.title: Want Safer Execution
docs.text:
Use Docker backend:

python examples/showcase_mcp_stdio.py --executor-backend docker

docs.title: Want Real LLM Optimization
docs.text:
export GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key_here
python examples/optimize_gemini_flash.py --max-metric-calls 4

docs.title: Where to Look Next
docs.text:
• Examples page for all runnable scripts
  • GEPA Adapter Changes page to understand what is optimized
  • Code Map page to understand each file
  • Troubleshooting page if something fails

docs.title: Cloudflare Guide
docs.text: This guide shows Cloudflare MCP direct usage and HTTP bridge usage.

docs.title: What Code Mode Is (short intro)
docs.text:
Code Mode is a code-first MCP usage pattern where the model works through a small tool surface (often discovery + execution) and generates code for multi-step actions.

Related references:

  • Cloudflare MCP Code Mode blog: https://blog.cloudflare.com/code-mode-mcp/
  • UTCP Code Mode implementation: https://github.com/universal-tool-calling-protocol/code-mode

docs.title: Fit for Cloudflare Users
docs.text:
Cloudflare users often have a two tool pattern:

  • search tool for capability discovery
  • execute tool for running code

SuperCodeMode improves the client side text that decides when to use each tool.

docs.title: Option A: Cloudflare MCP Direct
docs.text:
Use the public MCP endpoint:

  • https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp

Run showcase:

scm showcase --runner mcp-http

Run optimization:

scm optimize --runner mcp-http --max-metric-calls 10

Override endpoint if needed:

scm showcase --runner mcp-http --endpoint https://your-mcp-endpoint.example.com/mcp

docs.title: Expected Demo Output
docs.text:
For the current Cloudflare demo dataset, a run can show:

  • first case uses search and returns JSON tool metadata
  • second case uses execute and returns {"result": 42}
  • average score can be 0.5

This is expected with the current metric. The first example currently checks for the literal word search in final_answer, but Cloudflare may return structured JSON without that word. In that case integration is still working if:

  • selected_tool=search for case 1
  • selected_tool=execute for case 2
  • case 2 returns 42

docs.title: Option B: HTTP Bridge
docs.text:
Use this when you run your own bridge service.

Request fields expected by the bridge:

  • user_query
  • system_prompt
  • codemode_description
  • tool_alias_map
  • tool_description_overrides
  • additional_context

Response fields:

  • final_answer
  • generated_code
  • selected_tool
  • tool_calls
  • logs
  • error

Run showcase:

scm showcase --runner http --endpoint http://localhost:8080/run-codemode

Run optimization:

scm optimize --runner http --endpoint http://localhost:8080/run-codemode --max-metric-calls 10

Save artifact:

scm optimize --runner http --endpoint http://localhost:8080/run-codemode --save-artifact

docs.title: Why This Is Valuable Without Server Side Auto Optimization
docs.text:
You still get strong gains by improving:

  • tool routing text
  • planning instructions
  • output consistency
  • tool call quality

Server code can remain unchanged while client policy gets better.

For a detailed GEPA explanation, read guides/gepa-adapter.md.

docs.title: Examples Overview
docs.text: All runnable scripts are under examples/.

docs.title: Dataset Templates
docs.text:
Starter datasets:

  • datasets/templates/two_tool_minimal.jsonl
  • datasets/templates/cloudflare_search_execute.jsonl
  • datasets/templates/routing_mix.jsonl

docs.title: Local MCP Showcase
docs.text:
python examples/showcase_mcp_stdio.py

With artifact:

python examples/showcase_mcp_stdio.py --save-artifact

docs.title: Static Showcase
docs.text: python examples/showcase_static.py

docs.title: HTTP Showcase
docs.text: python examples/showcase_http.py --endpoint http://localhost:8080/run-codemode

docs.title: Cloudflare MCP Showcase
docs.text:
python examples/showcase_mcp_cloudflare.py
# or
scm showcase --runner mcp-http

docs.title: Local Optimization
docs.text: python examples/optimize_mcp_stdio.py --max-metric-calls 10

docs.title: HTTP Bridge Optimization
docs.text: python examples/optimize_cloudflare_http.py --endpoint http://localhost:8080/run-codemode --max-metric-calls 10

docs.title: Cloudflare MCP Optimization
docs.text:
python examples/optimize_mcp_cloudflare.py --max-metric-calls 10
# or
scm optimize --runner mcp-http --max-metric-calls 10

Expected behavior:

  • case 1 often selects search and returns structured metadata JSON
  • case 2 selects execute and returns {"result": 42}
  • average score may show 0.5 with current demo metric

docs.title: Gemini Low Cost Optimization
docs.text:
export GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key_here
python examples/optimize_gemini_flash.py --max-metric-calls 4

docs.title: Direct MCP Client Demo
docs.text: python examples/mcp_client_demo.py

docs.title: Add Observability to Any Command
docs.text: scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl showcase --runner mcp-stdio

docs.title: Understand What Is Being Optimized
docs.text: Read guides/gepa-adapter.md for a plain explanation of GEPA candidate fields, runner behavior, and transport choices.

docs.title: From Demo to Real Workflow
docs.text:
This guide explains how to use SuperCodeMode in your own workflow, not just run the built-in demos.

SuperCodeMode optimizes client-side Code Mode behavior with GEPA.

It does not replace your MCP server. It helps you improve how your client uses that server.

docs.title: Mental Model
docs.text:
SuperCodeMode sits between:

  • your evaluation loop (GEPA)
  • your Code Mode client behavior (prompting, routing guidance, tool naming)
  • your MCP runtime (Cloudflare, local MCP, UTCP, custom)

It optimizes the client-side behavior and keeps runtime transport/backend separate.

docs.title: What You Need to Bring
docs.text:
Before using SuperCodeMode for a real workflow, you need:

  1. An MCP server or Code Mode runtime
  2. Cloudflare MCP
  3. local MCP stdio server
  4. UTCP Code Mode

  5. internal/custom MCP server

  6. A small evaluation dataset (real tasks)

  7. 10 to 50 tasks is enough to start

  8. use tasks that reflect real production usage

  9. A scoring rule (metric)

  10. exact match
  11. contains required fields
  12. JSON validation

  13. custom pass/fail checks

  14. A place to apply the optimized result

  15. your agent client config
  16. your prompt template
  17. your Code Mode tool descriptions / aliases

docs.title: What SuperCodeMode Optimizes
docs.text:
SuperCodeMode is designed to optimize client-side Code Mode text and behavior such as:

  • system_prompt
  • codemode_description
  • tool_alias_map
  • tool_description_overrides

These fields affect:

  • whether the model discovers tools first
  • whether it chooses the right tool
  • how it plans execution
  • how stable the final output is

docs.title: What It Does Not Change
docs.text:
SuperCodeMode does not automatically:

  • modify your MCP server implementation
  • deploy server-side changes
  • change provider-side tool schemas

This is why it is useful even when you do not control the server.

docs.title: Step 1: Prove your runtime works (smoke test)
docs.text:
Use a built-in showcase first.

Local MCP:

scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio

Cloudflare MCP (requires auth in most environments):

scm showcase --runner mcp-http --auth-bearer "$CODEMODE_TOKEN"

Goal:

  • confirm transport works
  • confirm tool calls happen
  • confirm outputs and traces are produced

docs.title: Step 2: Benchmark baseline behavior
docs.text:
Run a quick comparison on the built-in dataset:

scm benchmark --runner mcp-stdio --save-artifact

This compares:

  • tool_call
  • codemode_baseline
  • codemode_optimized

Goal:

  • understand current behavior shape
  • inspect scores, tool calls, and failures
  • get familiar with summary artifacts

docs.title: Step 3: Replace the toy dataset with your tasks
docs.text:
For a real workflow, use your own tasks.

Good examples:

  • "Find the right API endpoint for X and return the path"
  • "Run a read-only audit and summarize results"
  • "Compute/report using the execution tool and return structured JSON"

Tips:

  • include easy, medium, and failure-prone tasks
  • include tasks where routing matters
  • include tasks where output format matters

docs.title: Step 4: Define a metric that reflects real success
docs.text:
Do not rely only on string contains checks for production workflows.

Use metrics like:

  • exact expected value match
  • JSON schema validation
  • required keys present
  • domain-specific pass/fail rules
  • custom evaluator script

The metric determines what GEPA optimizes toward.

docs.title: Step 5: Run optimization
docs.text:
After your dataset and metric are ready:

scm optimize --runner mcp-stdio --max-metric-calls 10 --save-artifact

For Cloudflare MCP:

scm optimize --runner mcp-http --auth-bearer "$CODEMODE_TOKEN" --max-metric-calls 10 --save-artifact

Goal:

  • generate improved client-side candidates
  • compare scores
  • inspect best candidate text

docs.title: Step 6: Apply the best candidate to your client
docs.text:
Take the optimized values (for example system_prompt, codemode_description, aliases, descriptions) and apply them to your real Code Mode client configuration.

This is the production handoff step.

docs.title: Step 7: Observe and iterate
docs.text:
Use observability and summary artifacts to track behavior changes over time.

Examples:

scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl benchmark --runner mcp-stdio
scm --obs-backend langsmith benchmark --runner mcp-stdio

docs.title: How to Interpret Results
docs.text:
Focus on more than just average score.

Also inspect:

  • selected_tool
  • tool call count
  • error count
  • error_categories
  • run summaries and benchmark summaries

A run can be useful even if scores are low, if it reveals routing mistakes, auth failures, or schema mismatches you can fix.

docs.title: Mistake 1: Expecting SuperCodeMode to change the server
docs.text: It optimizes the client-side behavior layer. Your MCP server remains the same unless you change it separately.

docs.title: Mistake 2: Using a toy metric for real tasks
docs.text: A weak metric teaches GEPA the wrong thing. Use metrics that reflect real success in your workflow.

docs.title: Mistake 3: Skipping runtime smoke tests
docs.text: If auth or MCP transport is broken, optimization results will be meaningless. Run showcase or doctor first.

docs.title: Mistake 4: Treating demo dataset scores as product benchmarks
docs.text: Built-in demos are smoke tests. Use your own tasks for meaningful optimization.

docs.title: Good Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Run scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio to verify local flow
  2. Run scm benchmark --runner mcp-stdio --save-artifact
  3. Build a small dataset from your real MCP tasks
  4. Define a metric that reflects real success
  5. Run scm optimize ... and apply the best candidate to your client

docs.title: Gemini Optimization Guide
docs.text: This guide runs real GEPA reflection optimization with Gemini Flash using small budget.

docs.title: Set API Key
docs.text: export GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key_here

docs.title: Run Low Cost Demo
docs.text: python examples/optimize_gemini_flash.py --max-metric-calls 4

docs.title: Docker Backend (Optional)
docs.text: python examples/optimize_gemini_flash.py --executor-backend docker --max-metric-calls 4

docs.title: Important Note
docs.text:
In adapter mode with current GEPA API:

  • task_lm is not used
  • reflection_lm drives optimization

This is expected behavior.

docs.title: Increase Quality If Needed
docs.text:
python examples/optimize_gemini_flash.py --max-metric-calls 12

Higher budgets can find better candidates but cost more.

docs.title: GEPA Adapter Changes
docs.text: This page explains the GEPA Code Mode adapter changes used by this project.

docs.title: What Code Mode Means Here
docs.text:
In this project, "Code Mode" means a code-first MCP tool orchestration pattern where the model uses a small tool surface (typically discovery + execution) and generates code to drive multi-step work.

Background:

  • Cloudflare blog (Code Mode + MCP context): https://blog.cloudflare.com/code-mode-mcp/
  • UTCP Code Mode implementation: https://github.com/universal-tool-calling-protocol/code-mode

docs.title: What Changed
docs.text:
The adapter keeps optimization logic in GEPA and keeps runtime execution in runner classes.

The runtime side now supports three patterns:

  1. MCPStdioCodeModeRunner
  2. local MCP servers over stdio

  3. good for UTCP and local development

  4. MCPStreamableHTTPCodeModeRunner

  5. direct MCP over streamable HTTP

  6. works with Cloudflare MCP endpoint https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp

  7. HTTPCodeModeRunner

  8. JSON bridge endpoint
  9. useful if your platform exposes non MCP HTTP contract

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Teams can optimize prompt and tool policy text once, while changing runtime transport without rewriting GEPA logic.

This makes contributions back to GEPA small:

  • adapter core stays simple
  • transport logic stays in runners
  • examples show local and Cloudflare paths

docs.title: What GEPA Optimizes
docs.text:
GEPA optimizes candidate text fields:

  • system_prompt
  • codemode_description
  • tool_alias_map (optional JSON string)
  • tool_description_overrides (optional JSON string)

GEPA does not mutate your server code.

docs.title: How Execution Works
docs.text:
For each dataset row:

  1. GEPA sends candidate text to the runner.
  2. Runner calls MCP or HTTP runtime tools.
  3. Runner returns:
  4. final_answer
  5. generated_code
  6. selected_tool
  7. tool_calls
  8. logs
  9. error
  10. Metric scores result.
  11. GEPA updates candidate text in optimization mode.

docs.title: Cloudflare MCP Flow
docs.text:
Use direct MCP transport:

scm showcase --runner mcp-http
scm optimize --runner mcp-http --max-metric-calls 10

mcp-http defaults to https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp. You can still override with --endpoint.

Cloudflare response note for this demo:

  • case 1 may return structured JSON from search (tool paths, counts)
  • case 2 should return {"result": 42} from execute
  • average score may be 0.5 with current string-match metric

Treat tool selection and response structure as the primary integration signal for this demo.

docs.title: Local MCP Flow
docs.text:
Use stdio transport:

scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio
scm optimize --runner mcp-stdio --max-metric-calls 10

docs.title: GEPA Example Scripts
docs.text:
If you are validating GEPA adapter behavior directly, use:

  • code_mode_mcp_cloudflare_example.py for Cloudflare MCP
  • code_mode_mcp_local_stdio_example.py for local stdio MCP

These are real transport examples, not static mocks.

docs.title: Local, Docker, and Monty Backends
docs.text: SuperCodeMode demo MCP server supports multiple execution backends.

docs.title: Local Backend
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio --executor-backend local

Best for speed and simple local demos.

docs.title: Docker Backend
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio --executor-backend docker

Best for safer code execution isolation.

docs.title: Monty Backend
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio --executor-backend monty

Best for a Python-native sandbox path without Docker.

Monty setup:

pip install "supercodemode[monty]"

If Monty is not installed, the demo server returns a clear executor error.

docs.title: Docker Requirements
docs.text:
• Docker daemon is running
  • your user can run docker run

Validate with:

docker info
docker run --rm python:3.12-alpine python -c "print(17+25)"

docs.title: Select Docker Image
docs.text:
scm showcase --runner mcp-stdio --executor-backend docker --docker-image python:3.12-alpine

Use a custom image if your org has approved base images.

docs.title: Operations
docs.text: This section is for production readiness and debugging.

docs.title: Core Operations Workflow
docs.text:
1. run doctor checks
  2. run showcase or optimization
  3. inspect artifacts and telemetry
  4. troubleshoot failures quickly

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Doctor
  • Observability
  • Troubleshooting

docs.title: Recommended Routine
docs.text:
scm doctor
scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl showcase --runner mcp-stdio --save-artifact

docs.title: Doctor Command
docs.text: scm doctor is the preflight command.

docs.title: What It Checks
docs.text:
• Python runtime
  • gepa import
  • mcp import
  • optional pydantic_monty import check (warn when missing)
  • Docker daemon reachability
  • optional Docker run check
  • MCP roundtrip check

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
scm doctor
scm doctor --json
scm doctor --strict
scm doctor --executor-backend monty
scm doctor --no-docker-run
scm doctor --no-mcp-roundtrip

docs.title: Exit Behavior
docs.text:
• default: exits 0, reports pass or fail in output
  • --strict: exits non zero if any warn or fail exists

Use strict mode in CI pipelines.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text: SuperCodeMode supports a common event schema with multiple backends.

docs.title: Backends
docs.text:
• none default
  • jsonl
  • otlp
  • logfire (optional SDK)
  • mlflow (optional SDK)
  • langsmith (optional SDK)
  • langfuse (optional SDK)

docs.title: JSONL Example
docs.text:
scm --obs-backend jsonl --obs-jsonl-path artifacts/obs.jsonl showcase --runner mcp-stdio

Example event fields include:

  • timestamp
  • run_id
  • event name
  • cli_command
  • cli_runner
  • cli_executor_backend
  • selected tool
  • tool_call_count
  • score
  • latency_ms
  • error

docs.title: OTLP Example
docs.text: scm --obs-backend otlp --obs-otlp-endpoint http://localhost:4318/v1/traces showcase --runner mcp-stdio

docs.title: Provider SDK Backends (optional)
docs.text:
Install provider integrations:

pip install "supercodemode[observability]"

Examples:

scm --obs-backend logfire showcase --runner mcp-stdio
scm --obs-backend mlflow showcase --runner mcp-stdio
scm --obs-backend langsmith benchmark --runner mcp-stdio
scm --obs-backend langfuse optimize --runner mcp-stdio --max-metric-calls 4

These adapters use the same SuperCodeMode event schema and are best-effort: SDK import/config errors will not fail the main run.

docs.title: Event Areas
docs.text:
Events are emitted from:

  • runners (static, mcp, http)
  • engine (showcase, optimize)
  • doctor command

Summary events are also emitted for:

  • run summaries (evaluate_candidate, optimize)
  • showcase comparison summary
  • benchmark summary

The benchmark and run summary payloads are designed for filtering/aggregation and include compact fields such as:

  • selected tool histograms
  • error taxonomy rollups (error_categories)
  • runner/executor context (cli_runner, cli_executor_backend)

See reference/telemetry-schema.md for the exact summary fields, runtime capability hints, and current error taxonomy labels.

docs.title: Summary Artifacts (with --save-artifact)
docs.text:
When you save CLI artifacts, SuperCodeMode also writes compact summary files for quick comparisons and CI-friendly diffs.

showcase writes:

  • *_comparison_summary_*.json
  • *_baseline_run_summary_*.json
  • *_tuned_run_summary_*.json

optimize writes:

  • *_run_summary_*.json

benchmark writes:

  • *_benchmark_summary_*.json
  • *_<variant>_run_summary_*.json

docs.title: Safety Rule
docs.text:
Telemetry failures do not fail the main run path.

This prevents observability outages from breaking optimization runs.

docs.title: Docker Permission Error
docs.text:
Symptom:

  • permission denied for Docker socket

Fix:

docker info
docker run --rm python:3.12-alpine python -c "print(17+25)"

Ensure your shell user can access Docker daemon.

docs.title: HTTP Runner Connection Error
docs.text:
Symptom:

  • urlopen error or connection refused

Fix:

  • verify endpoint URL and port
  • verify server accepts expected request and response contract
  • test endpoint with a simple curl before optimization run

docs.title: No Improvement In Optimization
docs.text:
Symptom:

  • best candidate remains baseline

Fix:

  • increase --max-metric-calls
  • use focused dataset templates
  • inspect artifacts and traces to see failure pattern

docs.title: Gemini Demo Fails
docs.text:
Symptom:

  • missing API key error

Fix:

export GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key_here

docs.title: GEPA API Version Mismatch
docs.text:
Symptom:

  • argument mismatch errors

Fix:

  • run scm doctor
  • reinstall latest gepa
  • use provided fallback adapter flow already in this repo
